title: Designing a Product System Prompt
concept: A worked example building a system prompt for a customer-support assistant with tone and scope constraints.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why do well-aligned AI models usually prioritize the system prompt over a conflicting user request?

options.text: Because the system prompt comes from the developer and sets trusted, higher-level rules the assistant should follow
Because user requests are always ignored completely
Because the system prompt is written in a special secret code

exercises.explain: The system prompt sets the foundational rules from the people building and deploying the app, so aligned models treat it as higher priority than everyday user requests — that's the instruction hierarchy we covered last lesson.

cards.title: Building the Perfect Helper
cards.body: Imagine you're hiring one assistant to answer every single customer message for your app, forever, without sleeping. What would you need to tell them on day one so they never go off-script?

cards.title: Product System Prompt
cards.body: A product system prompt is the instruction block a company writes once and reuses for every user, defining the assistant's role, tone, and scope so it behaves consistently across thousands of conversations.

cards.title: Like an Employee Handbook
cards.body: A good system prompt is like a new-hire handbook for a support agent: it sets the role ('You work for ShoeCo'), the tone ('friendly, patient'), and the boundaries ('never discuss competitor prices'). The employee (the model) uses this handbook to handle any customer who walks in.

cards.title: Building It Piece by Piece
cards.body: Role: 'You are a support assistant for CloudNote, a note-taking app.' Tone: 'Be warm, concise, and patient.' Scope: 'Only help with CloudNote features — do not discuss unrelated apps.' Fallback: 'If you don't know, say so and offer to connect them to a human agent.' Together these four pieces form a complete, working system prompt.

cards.title: Myth: Longer Is Always Better
cards.body: Piling on paragraph after paragraph doesn't automatically make a system prompt stronger. A bloated prompt with vague or repeated instructions can confuse the model. Clear, specific, well-organized instructions beat sheer length every time.

cards.title: Weak vs. Strong System Prompt
cards.body: Compare a vague instruction to a well-structured one for the same support bot.

right.text: 'You are ShoeCo's support assistant. Tone: friendly, concise. Scope: orders, returns, sizing only. Escalate billing disputes to a human.'

exercises.prompt: Which line best belongs in a customer-support system prompt to handle tricky questions safely?

options.text: 'Make up an answer if you're not sure, customers hate hearing I don't know.'
'If you don't know the answer, say so honestly and offer to escalate to a human agent.'
'Never say the word sorry under any circumstances.'
'Answer every question exactly the same way regardless of context.'

exercises.explain: Telling the assistant to admit uncertainty and escalate builds trust and avoids confidently wrong answers, which can seriously hurt a company's reputation. Making things up (option A) or refusing normal empathy (option C) creates a worse, less trustworthy support experience.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once a system prompt is written for a product, it should never be updated again.
exercises.explain: False — system prompts should be tested and refined over time as teams discover edge cases, new features launch, or the assistant misbehaves in unexpected ways. Treating it as a living document leads to better results than a 'set it and forget it' approach.

exercises.sentence: A good customer-support system prompt should define the assistant's role, tone, and scope so it stays consistent.
exercises.explain: Scope tells the assistant what topics it's allowed to talk about — for example, only app features, not unrelated subjects. This keeps the assistant focused and prevents it from wandering into risky or off-topic territory.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three system prompts was most likely written carelessly by an AI with no human review, rather than thoughtfully designed by a product team?

options.text: 'You are CloudNote's support assistant. Be warm and concise. Only discuss CloudNote features. If unsure, escalate to a human.'
'You are a helpful assistant that helps with things and answers questions and is nice and also helpful and answers things helpfully and is a helpful helper.'
'You are a support agent for TechBox. Tone: professional and patient. Scope: hardware troubleshooting only. Do not give warranty refund approvals — direct those to billing@techbox.com.'

exercises.explain: Option B repeats the word 'helpful' over and over without giving any real role, tone, or scope — a classic sign of unedited, low-effort AI filler text. Options A and C are specific, structured, and clearly reviewed by someone who thought about role, tone, scope, and edge cases.

exercises.prompt: You're building a system prompt for a bank's customer-support bot. Which combination of elements makes it strongest?

options.text: Just say 'Be helpful and answer banking questions.'

Define the role, set a professional-but-warm tone, list allowed topics (accounts, cards, transfers), explicitly forbid giving investment or legal advice, and state what to do when unsure (escalate to a human).
List every possible customer question and its exact scripted answer word-for-word.
Tell the model to say yes to whatever the customer asks so they stay happy.

exercises.explain: A strong system prompt combines several ingredients: a clear role, a defined tone, explicit scope (what topics are in vs. out of bounds), safety guardrails (no legal/financial advice it isn't qualified to give), and a fallback plan for uncertainty (escalate to a human). Option A is too vague to guide behavior. Option C is impossible to scale and breaks on any new question. Option D removes boundaries entirely, which is risky for a bank. This is the final lesson of the unit — great work! You now understand how system and user prompts work together to build safe, reliable AI products. 🎉 You've completed the LLM Application Engineering phase!
